title: RFID Smart Lock
subtitle: Tap In → Relay Clicks → Door Opens!
overview: Build a real working smart lock! An authorized RFID card makes a relay click and a servo motor swing the door open. Any other card gets denied — red LED, buzzer beep, and 'Access Denied' on the LCD. The same system used in offices, hotels, and secure labs — built by YOU on a breadboard.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — relay module, servo door model, wiring, access granted/denied demo.

gallery.caption: Same tech in hotel rooms, offices, and metro gates — now you built one!

keyTopics: Relay — An electrically controlled switch; Arduino (low power) controls a relay that switches higher-power devices
Normally Open (NO) vs Normally Closed (NC) — relay contacts: NO = circuit open until relay triggers
Servo Motor as Door — PWM signal from Arduino rotates servo to 0° (locked) or 90° (unlocked)
RFID UID Whitelist — a list of authorized card UIDs stored in Arduino code; tap = check against list
Access Granted Flow — UID matches → relay ON → servo opens → green LED → LCD 'Access Granted!'
Access Denied Flow — UID unknown → relay stays OFF → red LED → buzzer beeps → LCD 'Access Denied!'
Auto-Lock Timer — servo returns to 0° and relay OFF after 3 seconds using delay()
Real-World Applications — hotel key cards, office access control, school gates, ATM rooms, lab doors

codeExamples.title: Wiring Reference — Pin Connections
codeExamples.description: Wire everything up before uploading any code. Read the voltage warnings carefully!

codeExamples.title: Step 1 — Read Your Card's UID
codeExamples.description: Upload this first. Open Serial Monitor at 9600 baud, tap each authorized card, and write down its UID bytes. You'll paste them into Step 2.

codeExamples.title: Step 2 — Full RFID Smart Lock
codeExamples.description: The complete smart lock! Authorized card → relay clicks → servo opens door → green LED + LCD 'Access Granted!'. Unknown card → red LED + buzzer + LCD 'Access Denied!'. Auto-locks after 3 seconds.

codeExamples.title: How a Relay Works — Code Concept
codeExamples.description: A simple standalone sketch showing how to control a relay — understand it before wiring the full system.

codeExamples.title: Servo as Door — Angle Control
codeExamples.description: Understand servo angle control before adding it to the full smart lock circuit.

activity.title: Build a Working RFID Smart Lock with Relay + Servo Door
parts: MFRC522 RFID Reader + cards / key fobs (at least 2)
Cardboard (to build a mini door model)
Jumper wires + USB cable + Laptop with Arduino IDE
steps: Wire the RC522 to Arduino using 3.3V — NOT 5V! See wiring reference above.
Wire the relay module (IN → Pin 7, VCC → 5V, GND → GND).
Attach the servo to Pin 6. Hot-glue or tape servo arm to your cardboard door model.
Wire LCD I2C (SDA → A4, SCL → A5). Wire green LED → Pin 4, red LED → Pin 5, buzzer → Pin 8.
Upload Step 1 — scan each card, write down its UID bytes from Serial Monitor.
Paste UIDs + names into Step 2's authorized[] array. Upload Step 2.
Test: tap an authorized card → relay clicks → servo swings open → green LED + 'Access Granted!'
Test: tap an unknown card → 3 buzzer beeps → red LED → LCD 'Access Denied!'
Bonus Challenge: Add a keypad so users must also enter a PIN after tapping — two-factor access!

quiz.q: What is a RELAY in electronics?
options: A type of RFID card
An electrically controlled switch — a low-power signal controls a higher-power circuit
A wireless communication module
A type of resistor that changes value
quiz.explanation: A relay uses an electromagnet to physically snap a switch open or closed. Arduino's tiny 5V signal controls the relay coil, which then switches a bigger circuit — like a door lock solenoid!

quiz.q: What does 'Normally Open' (NO) mean on a relay?
options: The relay is always ON
The relay circuit is OPEN (broken) by default and CLOSES only when the relay is triggered
The relay is broken and needs fixing
The relay is open to any signal
quiz.explanation: Normally Open = circuit is open (disconnected) when the relay is OFF. When the relay triggers, the switch snaps CLOSED — completing the circuit. Perfect for a door lock that should stay locked by default!

quiz.q: In our smart lock code, what happens when an authorized card is tapped?
options: Red LED turns on and buzzer beeps 3 times
Nothing happens — the system waits
Relay turns ON, servo rotates to 90°, green LED glows, LCD shows 'Access Granted!'
Arduino resets automatically
quiz.explanation: Authorized card → isAuthorized() returns true → grantAccess() runs: relay LOW (ON), servo to 90° (door opens), green LED HIGH, LCD shows 'Access Granted! Welcome, [name]!' — then auto-locks after 3 seconds.

quiz.q: What happens when an UNKNOWN card is tapped?
options: The servo opens halfway
Green LED blinks slowly
Red LED + buzzer gives 3 short beeps, LCD shows 'Access Denied! Unknown User!'
The Arduino shuts down
quiz.explanation: denyAccess() runs: 3 short buzzer + red LED beeps, LCD shows 'Access Denied! Unknown User!'. Relay stays OFF so the door never unlocks. Security!

quiz.q: What angle does the servo rotate to when the door OPENS?
options: 0 degrees
180 degrees
45 degrees
90 degrees
quiz.explanation: doorServo.write(90) rotates the servo to 90° — the open position. doorServo.write(0) returns it to 0° — the locked position. After 3 seconds the code auto-locks by calling write(0) again.

quiz.q: Why does our relay use LOW to turn ON and HIGH to turn OFF?
options: It's a mistake in the code
Most relay modules are ACTIVE LOW — they activate when the signal pin is pulled LOW
Arduino can only output LOW signals
HIGH voltage would burn the relay
quiz.explanation: Most common relay breakout modules are active LOW — the internal transistor pulls the coil to ground when the signal pin is LOW, energising the electromagnet. Always check your module's datasheet!

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the UID whitelist (authorized[] array) in the code?
options: It stores the WiFi password
It is a list of approved card UIDs — only cards on this list get access
It controls the servo speed
It sets the LCD brightness
quiz.explanation: The authorized[] array is our approved list. Each entry holds a UID (4 bytes) and the owner's name. When a card is tapped, isAuthorized() checks if its UID matches any entry — only matches unlock the door.

quiz.q: Why does the code call rfid.PCD_StopCrypto1() at the end of loop()?
options: To encrypt the card data
To turn off the RFID reader permanently
To properly end the card communication session so the next tap is read cleanly
To save power
quiz.explanation: PCD_StopCrypto1() ends the encrypted communication session with the card. Without it, the reader may get stuck and fail to read the next card. Always pair it with PICC_HaltA() at the end of each read.

quiz.q: What voltage does the RC522 RFID module require — and why does it matter?
options: 5V — same as all other Arduino modules
12V — it needs extra power for radio transmission
3.3V — connecting to 5V will permanently damage the module
1.5V — it runs on a AA battery internally
quiz.explanation: The RC522 is a 3.3V module. Connecting VCC to Arduino's 5V pin will immediately damage or destroy it. Always use the Arduino's 3.3V pin for this module!

quiz.q: Which Arduino library controls the servo motor in our smart lock?
options: MFRC522.h
Wire.h
Servo.h
LiquidCrystal_I2C.h
quiz.explanation: Servo.h is Arduino's built-in servo library — no installation needed! It provides doorServo.attach(pin) to connect the servo and doorServo.write(angle) to set its position.

quiz.q: What is the auto-lock feature in our smart lock?
options: The door locks if no card is tapped for 1 hour
After 3 seconds of being open, servo returns to 0° and relay turns OFF automatically
The lock sends an email when someone is denied
The LCD turns off to save power after 10 minutes
quiz.explanation: After grantAccess() opens the door (servo 90°, relay ON), delay(3000) waits 3 seconds, then servo returns to 0° and relay goes HIGH (OFF) — auto-locking the door without anyone pressing a button!

quiz.q: What is the buzzer pattern when access is DENIED?
options: One long continuous beep
No sound — only the red LED activates
Three short beeps with red LED flashing in sync
A musical tune
quiz.explanation: The denyAccess() loop runs 3 times: red LED HIGH + buzzer HIGH for 200ms, then both LOW for 150ms. Three short sharp beeps with synchronized red flashes — a clear alarm signal!

quiz.q: In the smart lock, what do the COM and NO terminals of the relay connect to?
options: COM → Arduino GND, NO → Arduino 5V
COM → one side of the external load's power path, NO → the other side (circuit completes when relay triggers)
COM → relay signal pin, NO → buzzer
Both connect to Arduino pin 7
quiz.explanation: COM (Common) and NO (Normally Open) are the relay's output switch terminals. Wire your external load (like a door solenoid or bulb) between COM and NO. When the relay triggers, the switch closes and current flows through the load.

quiz.q: What is a TWO-FACTOR access system (the bonus challenge)?
options: Using two RFID cards at the same time
Having two relay modules in series
Requiring BOTH an authorized RFID card tap AND a correct PIN keypad entry to unlock
Installing two servo motors on one door
quiz.explanation: Two-factor = two separate checks. Factor 1: tap authorized RFID card. Factor 2: enter correct PIN on keypad. BOTH must pass to open the lock — much more secure! Banks and phones use this concept (card + PIN, phone + fingerprint).

quiz.q: Which real-world places use RFID smart lock technology?
options: Only science fiction movies
Only very expensive government buildings
Hotel rooms, office access gates, school labs, metro entry barriers, and ATM rooms
Only NASA spacecraft
quiz.explanation: RFID access control is everywhere! Hotel key cards, office turnstiles, school lab doors, metro tap gates, and ATM room locks all use the same core principle you just built — RFID UID check → relay → door mechanism.
